answers.intake_q1: When did your ear pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe the type of ear pain and if it is constant or comes and goes?
answers.intake_q3: Any hearing changes, ear discharge, or recent upper respiratory symptoms?
answers.intake_q4: Can you tell me more about the hearing changes, ear discharge, and recent upper respiratory symptoms you mentioned?
answers.intake_a4: coughing, sneezing, sore throat, fever, chills
answers.intake_q5: Can you describe the hearing changes and whether you have noticed any ear discharge?
answers.intake_q6: Have you noticed any ear discharge or fluid coming from the ear?
answers.intake_q7: Have you taken any medications or treatments for your symptoms so far, and if so, what and did they help?
answers.intake_q8: Have you experienced any dizziness, severe headache, or swelling around the ear?
answers.intake_q9: Any known allergies or chronic medical conditions?
answers.intake_q10: Have you noticed any difficulty swallowing or neck pain?

gating_notes: pregnancy status is yes/unknown (avoid teratogenic options unless explicitly appropriate)

note.soap:
S: The patient is a 14-year-old male presenting with a 4-day history of dull, intermittent ear pain. He reports associated upper respiratory symptoms including coughing, sneezing, sore throat, fever, and chills. He denies hearing changes, ear discharge, dizziness, severe headache, swelling around the ear, difficulty swallowing, or neck pain. He has not taken any medications or treatments for these symptoms so far. There are no known medication allergies or chronic medical conditions.

O: Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is acute otitis media likely secondary to an upper respiratory tract infection given the ear pain with systemic symptoms and absence of ear discharge or hearing loss.  
Differential:  
1. Acute otitis media  
2. Viral upper respiratory infection with referred ear pain  
3. Otitis externa (less likely without discharge or localized ear symptoms)  
4. Eustachian tube dysfunction or glue ear  

P:  
 - First-line medication: Amoxicillin 45 mg/kg/day divided into two doses for 10 days; avoid if allergic to penicillin (EVID_1, EVID_3). Renal and hepatic dose adjustments not specified; verify if needed.  
 - Alternatives:  
   - Amoxicillin-clavulanate 45 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin component) divided twice daily for 10 days; avoid if penicillin allergy (EVID_1).  
   - Cefdinir 14 mg/kg/day once daily or divided twice daily for 10 days; avoid if severe penicillin allergy (EVID_1).  
 - Medication safety considerations: No known allergies, no pregnancy concerns, no renal or liver disease reported. Confirm dosing if renal or hepatic impairment is later identified.  
 - Return precautions: Advise to seek in-person care if symptoms worsen, ear pain becomes severe, hearing loss develops, ear discharge appears, or if fever persists beyond 48–72 hours despite treatment.  
 - Testing: If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., otoscopic exam, tympanometry, or other workup as indicated).

evidence.title: Ear Infections | Otitis Media | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet:
ou 
Children 
Patient Handouts 
Summary 
Ear infections are the most common reason parents bring their child to a doctor. Three out of four children will have at least one ear infection by their third birthday. Adults can also get ear infections, but they are less common.
The infection usually affects the middle ear and is called otitis media. The tubes inside the ears become clogged with fluid and mucus. This can affect hearing, because sound cannot get through all that fluid.
If your child isn't old enough to say "My ear hurts," here are a few things to look for:
Tugging at ears 
Crying more than usual 
Fluid draining from the ear 
Trouble sleeping 
Balance difficulties 
Hearing problems 
Your health care provider will diagnose an ear infection by looking inside the ear with an instrument called an otoscope.
Often, ear infections go away on their own. Your health care provider may reco

velopment and progress at school.
Glue ear is much more common in children, but adults with glue ear have the same symptoms.
Other conditions that cause ear pain
Other causes of ear pain 
Symptoms
Possible condition
Ear pain with a change in hearing
Earwax build-up , an object stuck in the ear (do not try to remove it yourself – see a GP), perforated eardrum (particularly after a loud noise or accident)
Ear pain with toothache
Baby teething , dental abscess 
Ear pain with pain when swallowing
Sore throat , tonsillitis 
Ear pain with a high temperature
Ear infection , flu , cold 
Non-urgent advice: See a GP if:
Your child has signs of a hearin

evidence.title: Ear infections - NHS
evidence.snippet:
tting ear infections 
Urgent advice: Ask for an urgent GP appointment or get help from NHS 111 now if:
You or your child have symptoms of an ear infection and:
feel generally unwell have a very high temperature, or feel hot, cold or shivery have swelling around the ear fluid is coming out of the ear have a change in hearing or new hearing loss feel sick or are being sick (vomiting), feel dizzy or have a severe sore throat your child is under 12 months old and has earache in both ears have diabetes, or a heart, lung, kidney or neurological condition have a weakened immune system (for example, because of chemotherapy) You can call 111 or get he

Conditions A to Z
Otosclerosis 
Otosclerosis is a problem with the bones inside the ear which causes gradual hearing loss. Hearing aids or surgery will usually improve your hearing and total hearing loss is rare. 
Symptoms of otosclerosis 
Symptoms of otosclerosis include:
gradual hearing loss in 1 or both ears, often over several years hearing ringing, buzzing or other noises in 1 or both ears ( tinnitus ) dizziness problems with your balance Otosclerosis is a common cause of hearing loss in younger adults. Symptoms usually start between the ages of 30 to 50.
Non-urgent advice: See a GP if:
you think your hearing is gradually getting worse you have tinnitus regularly o
